rationale: A visible concentration of armored units and airpower along the border will deter Tethys from exploiting our economic vulnerability while buying time for economic countermeasures.
Disrupting Tethys’ energy infrastructure control systems would create leverage without crossing the kinetic threshold, asymmetrically offsetting their pipeline advantage.
Orchestrating a staged provocation in a contested zone could galvanize domestic support and justify escalation if necessary, aligning with nationalist sentiment.
Covertly degrading key nodes in Tethys’ energy transit infrastructure undermines their economic leverage and reduces their ability to withstand prolonged sanctions.
Strengthening internal security across defense and energy sectors mitigates Tethys’ growing ability to exploit our systemic vulnerabilities through espionage.
Providing select data on Tethys’ resistance networks to neutral powers builds goodwill and may induce indirect pressure on Tethys through third-party scrutiny.
Securing reserves of critical imports now will insulate the economy from imminent supply shocks and prevent panic-driven collapses during escalation.
Diversifying export routes through emergency talks reduces dependency on Tethys’ pipeline and weakens their sanction-induced stranglehold.
Temporarily managing exchange rates can stabilize inflation expectations and preserve domestic purchasing power amid reserve erosion.
Demonstrating resolve through visible troop movements and air patrols near the border will signal readiness without triggering escalation.
Disrupting Novaris military command systems temporarily degrades their coordination during mobilization.
Positioning naval assets in offshore zones secures maritime supply lines and deters amphibious threats.
Covertly disrupting Novaris’ energy export infrastructure creates leverage by targeting revenue without direct confrontation.
Providing select allies with verified data on Novaris vulnerabilities strengthens collective bargaining and counters disinformation.
Orchestrating a deniable incident that exposes Novaris’ aggression could shift international opinion in our favor.
Engaging neutral powers through discreet diplomacy preserves off-ramps while signaling openness to de-escalation.
Proposing third-party mediation publicly positions us as responsible actors despite rising tensions.
Establishing aid access points reinforces moral authority and draws contrast with Novaris’ militarization.
Securing critical reserves now mitigates collapse risks if trade is severed within the six-month window.
Targeted export controls on dual-use goods increase economic friction without triggering full retaliation.
Subtly weakening Novaris’ foreign exchange access through market operations undermines their sanction resilience.
